_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Smith County, TX. Record survival and courthouse history for the county of Tyler — commercial center of East Texas. No major fire losses; records continuous from 1846.

previous_courthouses.fate: Early frame courthouse. Replaced as Tyler grew.
Brick courthouse. No significant fire damage.

record_losses.description: Smith County's courthouse has not experienced a major fire or disaster. Records are continuous from county organization in 1846. Tyler's role as a regional commercial center means the county's records are among the best-maintained in East Texas.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Smith County's records are intact — if {ancestor_name} appears to be missing from the record, the explanation lies in the ancestor's circumstances (poverty, transience, illiteracy) rather than in courthouse destruction.
record_losses.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission. County Records Inventory.

vital_records.completeness: Better than rural counties due to Tyler's urban infrastructure, but still spotty before 1910.
vital_records.repository: Texas Department of State Health Services

substitute_sources.description: The Tyler Courier-Times (and predecessor titles) published from the 1870s. Obituaries, marriage notices, court proceedings, and local news supplement courthouse records.
substitute_sources.repository: Smith County Historical Society; East Texas Research Center, SFA; some Newspapers.com digitization
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for obituaries (detailed by 1900), social news, and legal notices. The newspaper's coverage of the oil boom period (1930s) is particularly rich.
substitute_sources.source: Smith County Historical Society finding aids.

substitute_sources.description: Tyler's commercial rose nurseries maintained employment and shipping records. Where these survive, they document the horticultural workforce that replaced the cotton labor force.
substitute_sources.repository: Smith County Historical Society; Tyler Public Library local history collection
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Documents a distinctive local workforce. Rose nursery records place families in a specific industry unique to Smith County.
substitute_sources.source: Tyler Chamber of Commerce. Tyler and Smith County, Texas: A History.

narrative_hooks: Smith County's courthouse records are complete from 1846 — {ancestor_name}'s deeds, marriages, and court appearances are almost certainly preserved.
Tyler's newspapers fill gaps between census years — the Tyler Courier-Times published obituaries, marriage notices, and court news that place {ancestor_name} in specific years and circumstances.
Texas vital records start late (1903, unreliable until ~1910) — for {ancestor_name}'s birth before that date, church records and census records are the standard sources in Smith County.
If {ancestor_name} worked in Tyler's rose nurseries, employment records may survive that document a workforce unique to this county — the people who made Tyler the rose capital.
source_references: Texas State Library and Archives Commission. County Records Inventory.
Smith County Historical Society finding aids.
Tyler Chamber of Commerce. Tyler and Smith County, Texas: A History. Tyler, 1976.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Texas County Records. Austin, 1937–1942.
